item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tend to not read the comments section of any SCP article until well after I've finished and decided what I believe it is about. But in the style and tone, I could already to some extent guess the author.
Brilliant, as always, WrongJohn. +1
In addition, the mental image of a skinless ancient corpse running around swapping hides with random locals is an incredibly disturbing one, even as an intentional "scary murder monster".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clichés put together like a classic. Not necessarily that it's of perfect quality (I'm with Petrograd above on the prose) but it seems like an old creepy story; good pieces put together well enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dang, I was planning to make a skip based on nábrók.

You totally still can! Heck, feel free to use this as an origin story.

And does he still look the same after his escape, since he left behind his "Zanzibar skin" (unless that was a copy of his real skin)?

Well, from the report:

Researcher Severskaya was seen in security footage to have run out of the observation chamber, scanned herself out of Building 17-C, and exited Site-19 under haste.

So he looked like Severskaya when he fled. Whether he still does now, or changed his skin, or whatever, can be left for further storytelling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
no, no. Agent Washington was naked. He was in the room with the SCP.

Researcher Severskaya was not naked. She was watching the interview remotely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote here. I think Wolf's journal was a little too heavy on the future-talk and very direct exposition. I still liked the setup and payoff with him, but the middle steps leading up to the double-cross (events observed by others in the audio log) were just a bit clunky, probably due to framing device. I dunno, it might've been clearer/worked better as an event timeline than dialogue?

I have no idea what the hell was going on at the end of the interview, which hurts that section.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seasonal Clothing-optional Plantation?
Picnic-area?
Pastoralism?

Finishing the backronym was always the hardest part for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh. I wonder if there's some way we can work the SCP initials into the name of a nudist camp?

Anyway it would seem prudent that the recapture and containment procedures for this scip should be extended slightly to include a) incapacitating it remotely, b) stripping it naked whilst knocked out, and keeping it unclothed whilst in containment, c) only interviewing it remotely, possibly using audio links only if video is too much of an ability-vectoring risk, d) possibly keeping it permanently sedated, seeing as it appears to have normal human susceptibility to being tased, so it may well be possible to keep it knocked out in a similar fashion (but using tranquilliser / coma-inducing drugs). Simple physical restraints might not be sufficient if it can alter form even without clothing, and we don't know the extent of its reality bending - after all, it was able to strip an agent of her skin even though she was herself naked. But it can't use either ability if unconscious… (GoC engagement rules, somewhat, but they seem valid).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Brief google shows that it may be "a fine fibre obtained from the leaves of pine trees", or a brand name for a type of blended yarn made from Merino wool and semi-synthetic spun-cellulose fibre.

However, the third option appears the most likely: Cotton. Its name in a lot of European languages, particularly Germanic and Scandinavian, either literally translates to "tree wool" (e.g. Baumwolle), or has an etymological base traceable back to it.

As it's one of the world's most common fabrics, and is otherwise missing from the list, it would also be the default assumption even without that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dang, I was planning to make a skip based on nábrók.

The log was a little confusing, possibly because most of the characters other than Arnfast and the skip weren't really distinguishable.

So was 4750 even in the interview room at all, or did he just leave behind his animated clothes and skin? And does he still look the same after his escape, since he left behind his "Zanzibar skin" (unless that was a copy of his real skin)?

There was piles of linen and leather and wool and the tree-wool all in sheets of fabric.

Should probably be "there were piles". Is "tree-wool" supposed to be cotton? Barkcloth?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The writing is top-notch and the villain is effective. I enjoy the intelligence he is treated with and his/your insertion of the philosophy of style and its social dynamics. The lack of presented logic regarding the need to have clothes on for the skinning to take place (unless I missed something) makes the all-nude staff come off as a bit forced (I would have liked to see a more…natural unfolding of this necessity), but it is highly entertaining to imagine. The ending was predictable, but hey, this is clichecon. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As with your 4242: this is a great skip that's killed by its ending. The final log is just unnecessary exposition that deflates all the tension and mystery for no readily apparent reason beyond the contest requirements.

Once again, a reluctant -1 from me. There is a lot of really good stuff here, again, but it just doesn't come together cohesively when taken as a whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I said this in chat, but yeeeeesh. Poor Researcher Severskaya. I think this is an excellent rendition of the tropes you chose, and it's a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While it's short, the entry log from the field agent and the two following logs do make it a more enjoyable read. And add more to what would seem initially like a bare bones SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They haven't been able to test the safe distance, yet, so they're not sure where it is yet. And since the SCP is at large, they're not in a position to test it yet.

So if the containment procedures seem unrefined, it's mainly because they haven't had enough opportunity to refine them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is tied with 3966 for my favorite WJS article. It's so uncomfortable to read, and the out-of-place nature of a Viking Foundation agent being involved makes it a new level of odd. And it gives it a surreal, 'bad trip' vibe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, even if they don't know exactly how his anomaly works, it wouldn't be that hard to figure out a maximum range (and if it doesn't have a maximum range, then confining him to a site wouldn't work).

I'm not saying the Foundation wouldn't have a nude site if they had too, but I think it would take a little more than this. Just stick the guy in a containment chamber and mark out a safe distance from it.

No vote. If they just had to be nude when within his presence, maybe I'd feel differently.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
So here's my entry into the 2019 Cliché Contest! Many thanks to Cyantreuse, The Great Hippo, and fieldstone for crit and encouragement.

Clichés I used for the contest:

  • Spooky scary murder monster!
  • An interview in which the anomaly is overly cryptic, followed by the interviewer suffering.

With sort of a half-entry into the "A highly skilled Mobile Task Force gets steamrolled" category.

Photos used are:

  • sewing by Georgie Pauwels (released under CC-BY 2.0)
  • Stone Town, Zanzibar by Rod Waddington (released under CC-BY-SA 2.0)
